subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Expanded National Government Powers and Policy Making
stimulus:
The United States Constitution establishes a federal system of government. Under federalism, policy making is shared between national and state governments. Over time, the powers of the national government have increased relative to those of the state governments.

Develop an argument about whether the expanded powers of the national government benefits or hinders policy making.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: The thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. It does NOT need to reference foundational documents — that requirement applies to the evidence row only. The thesis may be located anywhere in the response. A 'because' clause with at least one reason constitutes a line of reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Takes a clear position on whether expanded national power benefits or hinders policy making
Establishes a line of reasoning (provides at least one reason via 'because' clause or equivalent)
alternatives.correct_example: The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because it allows for more consistency across states and makes it easier to respond to large scale problems.

required_elements: Takes a clear position on whether expanded national power benefits or hinders policy making
Establishes a line of reasoning (provides at least one reason via 'because' clause or equivalent)
alternatives.correct_example: The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because of the strength of the Constitution, the increase of cooperative federalism, and the advantages of fiscal federalism.

wrong_examples: The federal government has expanded power over time. (Factual statement, no position on benefits/hinders)
Because of my knowledge of United States Government, I believe the expanded powers benefit policy making. (No line of reasoning — just assertion)
There are pros and cons to expanded national power. (No clear position taken)
common_traps: Do NOT require foundational documents in the thesis — documents are only required in the evidence row
A simple 'because X and Y' clause IS a line of reasoning — do not require sophistication
Restating the prompt without adding reasoning does not earn the point

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of evidence. One must come from a listed foundational document (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10). A second piece can come from any foundational document or course concept.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: The student earns 0, 1, 2, OR 3 points — award the HIGHEST tier met, not each independently. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A (thesis). Tier 3 requires one piece from a listed foundational document.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic (does not need to support the thesis directly)
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 10, the Commerce Clause, or another relevant concept.

wrong_examples: Evidence is inaccurate or completely irrelevant to the prompt topic
common_traps: At tier 1, evidence only needs to be relevant — it does not need to directly support the thesis

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. (Requires Row A thesis earned.)

required_elements: One specific piece of evidence that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces of evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 10 to argue that a large republic controls factions, supporting the claim that expanded national power benefits policy making.

wrong_examples: Lists evidence without connecting it to the thesis
common_traps: This tier requires Row A (thesis) — if thesis is 0, cap evidence at tier 1

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, at least one from a listed foundational document. (Requires Row A thesis earned.)

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one piece from Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 10 to argue large republics control factions AND uses the Commerce Clause to show how national regulation creates uniform economic policy — both supporting the thesis.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence mentioned but neither from a listed foundational document
Evidence from foundational document but does not support the thesis
common_traps: The foundational document must be from the LISTED documents (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10)
Both pieces must specifically support the thesis, not just be relevant to the topic

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis) and at least 2 points in Row B (evidence). Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the claim — not just restate it.

scoring_points.description: Reasoning: Use reasoning to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Explains the logical connection between evidence and thesis (how or why)
Goes beyond restating the evidence — must show cause-effect or analytical reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The Articles of Confederation made it harder to pass laws because each state had different agendas and required a supermajority to agree, making it nearly impossible to change policy. This shows that expanded national power improves policy making by reducing gridlock.

wrong_examples: Simply listing evidence without explaining the connection to the thesis
Restating the thesis without using evidence to support it
common_traps: Requires Row A (thesis) and at least tier 2 evidence (Row B >= 2pts)
The explanation must connect at least one piece of evidence to the claim

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis). Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut, refute, or concede it. Simply identifying an opposing view without responding to it does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Alternate Perspective: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.

required_elements: Describes an alternate or opposing perspective
Rebuts, refutes, or concedes the opposing perspective with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some argue that expanded national power hinders policy making because it ignores local needs, as Brutus 1 warns. However, the system of federalism still allows states to make policies in areas like education, so the national government's expanded power supplements rather than replaces state authority.

wrong_examples: Some people disagree. (Acknowledges opposition without rebutting)
The opposite of my thesis is wrong. (No actual engagement with the opposing argument)
common_traps: Requires Row A (thesis) — if thesis is 0, rebuttal cannot earn the point
Must do MORE than just identify the opposing view — must respond to it
